lesson.title: DNS and DHCP
lesson.objective: Explain the Domain Name System and how it resolves names to IP addresses
Describe DHCP and how it automatically assigns IP configuration
Trace a DNS resolution from client to authoritative server
lesson.definition: A hierarchical distributed naming system that translates human-readable domain names (e.g., www.example.com) into IP addresses (e.g., 93.184.216.34).
lesson.note: DNS is often called the "phone book of the internet." Without it, you'd need to memorize IP addresses instead of domain names.
lesson.example: When you type www.example.com in your browser: 1. Your computer checks its local DNS cache 2. If not cached, asks your ISP's Recursive Resolver 3. Resolver asks the Root Name Server (.) for .com TLD server 4. Root server refers to the .com TLD Name Server 5. TLD server refers to example.com's Authoritative Server 6. Authoritative server returns the IP address 7. Resolver caches the result and returns it to you
lesson.diagram: Client → Recursive Resolver → Root NS (.) → TLD NS (.com) → Authoritative NS (example.com) ← IP: 93.184.216.34 Client ← 93.184.216.34
lesson.definition: A Record: Maps hostname to IPv4 address AAAA Record: Maps hostname to IPv6 address CNAME: Alias pointing to another hostname MX Record: Mail server for a domain TXT Record: Text data (used for SPF, DKIM, etc.)
lesson.note: DNS uses UDP port 53 for queries (fast, lightweight) and TCP port 53 for large responses or zone transfers.
lesson.definition: A network protocol that automatically assigns IP addresses and network configuration (subnet mask, default gateway, DNS servers) to devices joining a network.
lesson.example: DHCP uses a 4-step process called DORA: 1. Discover: Client broadcasts "I need an IP address!" 2. Offer: DHCP server responds "I can give you 192.168.1.50" 3. Request: Client broadcasts "I'll take 192.168.1.50!" 4. Acknowledge: Server confirms "It's yours for 24 hours"
lesson.codeblock: DHCP DORA Process: Client DHCP Server |---DHCPDISCOVER (bcast)--->| |<--DHCPOFFER (unicast)-----| offers: 192.168.1.50 |---DHCPREQUEST (bcast)---->| requests: 192.168.1.50 |<--DHCPACK (unicast)-------| lease granted: 24h
lesson.note: DHCP leases expire. When a lease expires, the device must renew it or it loses its IP address. Servers and printers should use static IP addresses to ensure consistent accessibility.

question.text: What type of DNS record maps a domain name to an IPv4 address?
What does the 'D' in DHCP DORA stand for?
DHCP uses TCP for all communications.

lesson.exercise: Open a command prompt and run: (a) nslookup google.com — observe the DNS resolution (b) ipconfig /all (Windows) or ip addr (Linux) — note your DHCP-assigned IP, subnet mask, gateway, and DNS servers (c) ipconfig /displaydns — view your local DNS cache

summary.item: DNS translates domain names to IP addresses using a hierarchical distributed system
DNS resolution: Local cache → Recursive Resolver → Root NS → TLD NS → Authoritative NS
Common DNS records: A (IPv4), AAAA (IPv6), CNAME (alias), MX (mail), TXT
DHCP automatically assigns IP addresses using DORA: Discover, Offer, Request, Acknowledge
Use static IPs for servers; DHCP for client devices
